long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Multiple reaction steps where each step's product becomes the reactant for the next

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions asking 'how many' compounds satisfy a given property, or enumeration of specific cases

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms beyond surface-level recall - intermediates, transition states, electron flow

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties like acidity, basicity, electrophilicity, nucleophilicity, stability, bond strength, etc.

questions.question_text: The correct order of hydration enthalpies is: (A) K⁺ (B) Rb⁺ (C) Mg²⁺ (D) Cs⁺ (E) Ca²⁺
questions.solution_summary: Hydration enthalpy ∝ charge density (charge/radius). Mg²⁺ > Ca²⁺ > K⁺ > Rb⁺ > Cs⁺. Order: C > E > A > B > D.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison based on charge density.

questions.question_text: Number of cyclic tripeptides formed with 2 amino acids A and B is: (1) 3 (2) 2 (3) 4 (4) 5
questions.solution_summary: With 2 amino acids A and B forming cyclic tripeptides, the possible sequences are AAB and BBA only. So 2 cyclic tripeptides.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires combinatorial counting of cyclic peptide sequences.

questions.question_text: Which of the given compounds can enhance the efficiency of hydrogen storage tank? (1) Li/P₄ (2) NaNi₅ (3) SiH₄ (4) Di-isobutylaluminium hydride
questions.solution_summary: Metal alloys like NaNi₅, Ti-TiH₂, Mg-MgH₂ are used for hydrogen storage. NaNi₅ can absorb hydrogen reversibly.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of hydrogen storage materials.

questions.question_text: Match List-I with List-II: List-I (Antimicrobials): A. Narrow spectrum antibiotic B. Antiseptic C. Disinfectants D. Broad spectrum antibiotic. List-II (Names): I. Furacin II. Sulphur dioxide III. Penicillin G IV. Chloramphenicol.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Chemistry in Everyday Life
questions.solution_summary: Penicillin G → Narrow spectrum antibiotic (A-III); Furacin → Antiseptic (B-I); Sulphur dioxide → Disinfectants (C-II); Chloramphenicol → Broad spectrum antibiotic (D-IV).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Matching of antimicrobials with examples - pure recall.

questions.question_text: During the borax bead test with CuSO₄, a blue green colour of the bead was observed in oxidising flame due to the formation of: (1) CuO (2) Cu(BO₂)₂ (3) Cu₃B₂ (4) Cu
questions.solution_summary: Borax on heating forms NaBO₂ and B₂O₃. B₂O₃ + CuO → Cu(BO₂)₂ (cobalt(II) metaborate) which gives blue colour in oxidising flame.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of borax bead test reaction.

questions.question_text: The major product 'P' for the following sequence of reactions is:
questions.solution_summary: Phthalimide derivative undergoes Clemmensen reduction (Zn-Hg/HCl) followed by LiAlH₄ reduction to give the product P.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Multi-step reaction sequence involving Clemmensen and LiAlH₄ reductions.

questions.question_text: Identify the correct order for the given property for following compounds: (A) Boiling Point: CH₃Cl < CH₃Br < CH₃I (B) Density: CH₃Br < CH₃Cl < CH₃I (C) Boiling Point: n-butyl chloride > isobutyl chloride > tert-butyl chloride (D) Density: CH₃Br < CH₃I < CH₃Cl (E) Boiling Point: tert-butyl chloride > isobutyl chloride > n-butyl chloride
questions.solution_summary: A: Correct (boiling point increases with molar mass). B: Correct (density increases with molar mass). C: Correct (boiling point decreases with branching). D: Incorrect. E: Correct (branching decreases boiling point). So A, B, C and E are correct. No option contains all four.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of boiling point and density trends.

questions.question_text: 'A' obtained by Ostwald's method involving air oxidation of NH₃, upon further air oxidation produces 'B'. 'B' on hydration forms an oxoacid of Nitrogen along with evolution of 'A'. The oxoacid also produces 'A' and gives positive brown ring test. Identify A and B respectively.
questions.solution_summary: 4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O (A = NO). 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂ (B = NO₂). 4NO₂ + 2H₂O + O₂ → 4HNO₃ (oxoacid). HNO₃ gives brown ring test and produces NO.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of Ostwald's process and nitrogen oxides.

questions.question_text: The standard electrode potential (M³⁺/M²⁺) for V, Cr, Mn & Co are -0.26 V, -0.41 V, +1.57 V and +1.97 V, respectively. The metal ions which can liberate H₂ from a dilute acid are: (1) V²⁺ and Cr²⁺ (2) V²⁺ and Mn²⁺ (3) Cr²⁺ and Co²⁺ (4) Mn²⁺ and Co²⁺
questions.solution_summary: Metal ions with E° < 0 can reduce H⁺ to H₂. V²⁺/V³⁺ (-0.26 V) and Cr²⁺/Cr³⁺ (-0.41 V) have negative potentials and can liberate H₂ from dilute acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of reduction potentials.

questions.question_text: Match List-I with List-II: List-I (Reactions): A. Hoffmann Degradation B. Clemmensen reduction C. Cannizzaro reaction D. Reimer-Tiemann Reaction. List-II (Reagents): I. Conc. KOH, Δ II. CHCl₃, NaOH/H₃O⁺ III. Br₂, NaOH IV. Zn-Hg/HCl.
questions.solution_summary: Hoffmann Degradation → Br₂, NaOH (A-III); Clemmensen reduction → Zn-Hg/HCl (B-IV); Cannizzaro reaction → Conc. KOH, Δ (C-I); Reimer-Tiemann Reaction → CHCl₃, NaOH/H₃O⁺ (D-II).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Matching of named reactions with reagents - pure recall.

questions.question_text: The magnetic behavior of Li₂O, Na₂O₂ and KO₂, respectively, are: (1) Paramagnetic, paramagnetic and diamagnetic (2) Diamagnetic, diamagnetic and paramagnetic (3) Paramagnetic, diamagnetic and paramagnetic (4) Diamagnetic, paramagnetic and diamagnetic
questions.solution_summary: Li₂O (all paired electrons → diamagnetic). Na₂O₂ (O₂²⁻ → all paired → diamagnetic). KO₂ (O₂⁻ has unpaired electron → paramagnetic).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of magnetic properties of oxides and superoxides.

questions.question_text: The bond dissociation energy is highest for: (1) Cl₂ (2) I₂ (3) F₂ (4) Br₂
questions.solution_summary: Bond dissociation energy order: Cl₂ > Br₂ > F₂ > I₂. Cl₂ has the highest bond dissociation energy among halogens.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of bond dissociation energy trend in halogens.

questions.question_text: The shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is λ. The longest wavelength in Balmer series of He⁺ is: (1) 5/9λ (2) 5λ/9 (3) 36λ/5 (4) 9λ/5
questions.solution_summary: Shortest wavelength in Lyman series for H: 1/λ = R. Longest wavelength in Balmer for He⁺: n₁=2, n₂=3 → 1/λ' = R×4×(1/4-1/9) = 4R×5/36 = 5R/9. λ' = 9/(5R) = 9λ/5.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Rydberg formula for spectral lines.

questions.question_text: The reaction representing the Mond process for metal refining is: (1) Zr + 2I₂ → ZrI₄ (2) Ni + 4CO → Ni(CO)₄ (3) ZnO + C → Zn + CO (4) 2K[Au(CN)₂] + Zn → K₂[Zn(CN)₄] + 2Au
questions.solution_summary: Mond process is used for purification of Ni: Ni + 4CO (330-350K) → Ni(CO)₄. The carbonyl decomposes at 450-470K to pure Ni.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of Mond process for nickel refining.

questions.question_text: Chiral complex from the following is: (en ≡ ethylene diamine) (1) trans-[Co(NH₃)₄Cl₂] (2) cis-[PtCl₂(en)₂]²⁺ (3) cis-[PtCl₂(NH₃)₂] (4) trans-[PtCl₂(en)₂]²⁺
questions.solution_summary: cis-[PtCl₂(en)₂]²⁺ is chiral (optically active) as it has no plane of symmetry. It exists as a pair of enantiomers.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of chirality in coordination compounds.

questions.question_text: Compound that will give positive Lassaigne's test for both nitrogen and halogen is: (1) CH₃NH₂·HCl (2) NH₂OH·HCl (3) NH₄Cl (4) N₂H₄·HCl
questions.sub_topic: Lassaigne's test for N and halogens
questions.solution_summary: CH₃NH₂·HCl contains both N (amine) and Cl (chloride). In Lassaigne's test, it forms NaCN (for N) and NaCl (for Cl), giving positive tests for both.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of Lassaigne's test applicability.

questions.question_text: Which of the following salt solutions would coagulate the colloid solution formed when FeCl₃ is added to NaOH solution, at the fastest rate? (1) 10 mL of 0.1 mol dm⁻³ Ca₃(PO₄)₂ (2) 10 mL of 0.2 mol dm⁻³ AlCl₃ (3) 10 mL of 0.1 mol dm⁻³ Na₂SO₄ (4) 10 mL of 0.15 mol dm⁻³ CaCl₂
questions.solution_summary: FeCl₃ + NaOH forms a negative sol (Fe(OH)₃). Coagulation power ∝ charge of cation. Al³⁺ (highest charge) will coagulate fastest. Also higher concentration of AlCl₃ (0.2M) compared to others.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of coagulating power based on charge and concentration.

questions.question_text: The increasing order of pKₐ for the following phenols is: (A) 2,4-Dinitrophenol (B) 4-Nitrophenol (C) 2,4,5-Trimethylphenol (D) Phenol (E) 3-Chlorophenol
questions.solution_summary: Acidity order: A > B > E > D > C. Therefore pKₐ order (higher pKₐ = weaker acid): C > D > E > B > A. So A < B < E < D < C.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of acidity based on substituent effects.

questions.question_text: Correct statement about smog is: (1) NO₂ is present in classical smog (2) Classical smog also has high concentration of oxidizing agents (3) Photochemical smog has high concentration of oxidizing agents (4) Both NO₂ and SO₂ are present in classical smog
questions.solution_summary: Photochemical smog has high concentration of oxidizing agents (O₃, PAN, etc.). Classical smog has reducing agents (SO₂, etc.).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of smog types and their compositions.

questions.question_text: For 1 mol of gas, the plot of pV vs. p is shown below. What is the value of compressibility factor at point A?
questions.sub_topic: Compressibility factor - van der Waals gas
questions.solution_summary: At point A, 'a' is considerable and 'b' is negligible. Using van der Waals equation: (P + a/V²)V = RT → PV + a/V = RT → PV = RT - a/V. Z = PV/RT = 1 - a/(RTV).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of compressibility factor and van der Waals equation.

questions.question_text: Millimoles of calcium hydroxide required to produce 100 mL of the aqueous solution of pH 12 is x × 10⁻¹. The value of x is (Nearest integer).
questions.solution_summary: pH = 12 → pOH = 2 → [OH⁻] = 10⁻² M. For Ca(OH)₂, [OH⁻] = 2[Ca(OH)₂]. [Ca(OH)₂] = 5×10⁻³ M. In 100 mL: moles = 5×10⁻³ × 0.1 = 5×10⁻⁴ mol = 0.5 mmol = 5 × 10⁻¹ mmol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: pH to concentration conversion for a strong base.

questions.question_text: Solid Lead nitrate is dissolved in 1 litre of water. The solution was found to boil at 100.15°C. When 0.2 mol of NaCl is added to the resulting solution, it was observed that the solution froze at -0.8°C. The solubility product of PbCl₂ formed is ____ × 10⁻⁶ at 298 K. (Nearest integer). Given: K_b = 0.5 K kg mol⁻¹ and K_f = 1.8 K kg mol⁻¹. Assume molality to be equal to molarity in all cases.
questions.solution_summary: From boiling point: ΔTb = iKb m. 0.15 = 3×0.5×m → m = 0.1 M (Pb(NO₃)₂). After adding NaCl: Pb(NO₃)₂ + 2NaCl → PbCl₂ + 2NaNO₃. Remaining Pb²⁺ = (0.1 - s), Cl⁻ = (0.2 - 2s). Freezing point depression gives s = 0.0148. Ksp = 4s³ = 4(0.0148)³ ≈ 13×10⁻⁶.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Complex problem combining colligative properties and solubility product.

questions.question_text: Water decomposes at 2300 K: H₂O(g) → H₂(g) + ½O₂(g). The percent of water decomposing at 2300 K and 1 bar is ____ (Nearest integer). Equilibrium constant for the reaction is 2 × 10⁻³ at 2300 K.
questions.solution_summary: H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂. Let α = degree of dissociation. Total moles = 1 + α/2. P_total = 1 bar. Kp = [α/(1+α/2)] × [(α/2)/(1+α/2)]^(1/2). For small α: Kp ≈ α^(3/2)/√2. 2×10⁻³ = α^(3/2)/√2 → α^(3/2) = 2√2×10⁻³ → α ≈ 0.02 → 2%.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Kp expression to find degree of dissociation.

questions.question_text: The number of molecules or ions from the following, which do not have odd number of electrons are: (A) NO₂ (B) ICl₄⁻ (C) BrF₃ (D) ClO₂ (E) NO₂⁺ (F) NO
questions.sub_topic: Odd electron species - molecular orbital theory
questions.solution_summary: Odd electron species: NO₂ (odd), ClO₂ (odd), NO (odd). Even electron species: ICl₄⁻, BrF₃, NO₂⁺. So 3 species do not have odd electrons.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of odd electron species.

questions.question_text: Following figure shows dependence of molar conductance of two electrolytes on concentration. The number of incorrect statement(s) from the following is: (A) Λ°m for electrolyte A is obtained by extrapolation. (B) For electrolyte B, Λm vs √c graph is a straight line with intercept equal to Λ°m. (C) At infinite dilution, the value of degree of dissociation approaches zero for electrolyte B. (D) Λ°m for any electrolyte A or B can be calculated using λ° for individual ions.
questions.sub_topic: Molar conductivity - strong vs weak electrolytes
questions.solution_summary: A: False (Λ°m for weak electrolyte A cannot be obtained by extrapolation). B: True (Kohlrausch's law for strong electrolyte B). C: False (degree of dissociation approaches 1 at infinite dilution, not zero). D: True (Kohlrausch's law of independent migration). So 2 incorrect statements (A and C).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of strong vs weak electrolyte behaviour.

questions.question_text: For certain chemical reaction X → Y, the rate of formation of product is plotted against the time. The number of correct statement/s from the following is: (A) Overall order of this reaction is one. (B) Order of this reaction can't be determined. (C) In region I and III, the reaction is of first and zero order respectively. (D) In region-II, the reaction is of first order. (E) In region-II, the order of reaction is in the range of 0.1 to 0.9.
questions.sub_topic: Order of reaction from rate vs time graph
questions.solution_summary: Statements C and E are correct. The reaction shows different orders in different concentration regions (negative order in region I, fractional order in region II, zero order in region III).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires interpretation of rate-concentration graph for reaction order.

questions.question_text: The sum of bridging carbonyls in W(CO)₆ and Mn₂(CO)₁₀ is ____.
questions.sub_topic: Metal carbonyls - bridging vs terminal CO
questions.solution_summary: W(CO)₆ has all terminal CO ligands (no bridging). Mn₂(CO)₁₀ has all terminal CO ligands (no bridging). So sum of bridging carbonyls = 0.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of metal carbonyl structures.

questions.question_text: Following chromatogram was developed by adsorption of compound 'A' on a 6 cm TLC glass plate. Retardation factor of the compound 'A' is ____ × 10⁻¹.
questions.solution_summary: Rf = distance travelled by solute / distance travelled by solvent = 3/5 = 0.6 = 6 × 10⁻¹.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Simple calculation of Rf value from TLC data.

questions.question_text: Consider the following reaction approaching equilibrium at 27°C and 1 atm pressure: A + B ⇌ C + D. The standard Gibbs energy change (ΔrG°) at 27°C is (-) ____ kJ mol⁻¹. (Nearest Integer). (Given: R = 8.3 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ and ln 10 = 2.3)

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Gibbs free energy equation from equilibrium constant.

questions.question_text: 17 mg of a hydrocarbon (M.F. C₁₀H₁₆) takes up 8.40 mL of the H₂ gas measured at 0°C and 760 mm of Hg. Ozonolysis of the same hydrocarbon yields... The number of double bonds/s present in the hydrocarbon is ____.
questions.solution_summary: Moles of hydrocarbon = 17×10⁻³/136 = 1.25×10⁻⁴ mol. Moles of H₂ = (8.4×10⁻³)/(22.4) = 3.75×10⁻⁴ mol. Ratio = 3:1, so 3 double bonds (or one triple + one double, etc.). Total DOU = 3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Stoichiometric calculation from hydrogenation data.
